questionText: State the domain and range of $y = \arccos x$ and of $y = \arctan x$.

steps.explanation: To make cosine one-to-one it is restricted to the interval from $0$ to $\pi$ (that is $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$), where it decreases steadily from $1$ to $-1$.

steps.description: Read off the domain of arccos
steps.explanation: The inputs of $\arccos$ are the possible values of $\cos x$, which lie between $-1$ and $1$; no number outside this range has a cosine.

steps.description: Read off the range of arccos
steps.explanation: The outputs are the restricted angles, so $\arccos$ returns a single angle between $0$ and $\pi$ — note this is a different interval from arcsin.
Tangent is restricted to the open interval between $-\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$; the ends are excluded because tangent has asymptotes there.

steps.description: Read off the domain of arctan
steps.explanation: Tangent stretches out to every real value, so its inverse accepts every real number — there is no restriction on the input of $\arctan$.

steps.description: Read off the range of arctan
steps.explanation: The outputs are the restricted angles, strictly between $-\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$. The strict inequalities reflect that these values are approached but never reached.
Gathering everything: arccos maps $[-1,1]$ onto $[0,\pi]$, while arctan maps all of $\mathbb{R}$ onto the open interval $(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2})$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $y=\arccos x$: domain $-1 \le x \le 1$, range $0 \le y \le \pi$. $y=\arctan x$: domain all real $x$, range $-\tfrac{\pi}{2} < y < \tfrac{\pi}{2}$.
